actions.thought: Pocket fours are a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot. Raising from the button is a standard aggressive play.

In this Heads-Up scenario, I am in the Big Blind with 9h 7d (97o) facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. The pot odds require me to call 200 to win a total pot of 600, needing roughly 33% equity. While 97o has decent raw equity against a wide opening range (approx. 40-44%), it suffers significantly from poor playability out of position, especially being unsuited. Against a 2x or 2.5x open, a defense might be justifiable, but against a 3x sizing, 97o is generally considered a fold due to reverse implied odds and difficulty realizing equity. As this is the first hand, playing tight and avoiding marginal spots OOP is a solid default strategy.

I am on the Button (SB) in a heads-up match with 9s6s. This is a suited two-gapper with decent playability. In heads-up play, the Button should open a very wide range of hands (often 80%+) to assert initiative and leverage position post-flop. 96s is certainly strong enough to open. A min-raise to 200 is a standard, low-risk sizing that forces the Big Blind to defend while keeping the pot manageable relative to stack depths.

94o is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind, even with the pot odds of 3:1. Since I will be out of position post-flop, it is better to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot rather than potentially losing more chips with a marginal holding.
T7o is a playable hand from the button in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take advantage of my positional advantage, consistent with my sizing in the first hand.

I am in the Big Blind with KcTh facing a 3bb open (300 chips) from the Button/SB. In heads-up play, KTo is a strong starting hand, well ahead of a typical wide button opening range (often 70%+ of hands). I need to call 200 chips to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. KTo has approximately 57-58% equity against a wide opening range. While a 3-bet is an option, calling is standard to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid bloating the pot out of position against a range that likely continues only when it beats me. I will call to see a flop.

I am out of position on a dry paired board (5c 4h 5d) with King-high (KcTh). Standard strategy for the Big Blind against a pre-flop raiser is to check 100% of my range to the aggressor. My hand has showdown value against a wide button opening range, but betting would likely only fold out hands I am already beating (worse high cards) and isolate me against pairs or better high cards. Checking allows the opponent to bluff with worse hands or check back, keeping the pot manageable.

The board 5-4-5 is dry and favors the pre-flop aggressor. I have two overcards and since my opponent checked, I will place a continuation bet to represent a strong hand or simply to take the pot down immediately. Half pot is a standard sizing here.

I am facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600) on a dry, paired board of 5c 4h 5d. Holding King-Ten offsuit (KcTh), I have strong overcards and legitimate showdown value against a wide button opening range. The opponent's range includes many missed hands (Q-high, J-high, connectors) that they might c-bet with high frequency on this texture. I need 25% equity to call (300 to win 1200), and my hand likely has more than that against their entire range. Folding K-high here would be over-folding, and raising would likely only isolate me against better hands. Floating the flop to re-evaluate on the turn is the standard line.

The board is now double-paired (5c 4h 5d 4s). My hand is effectively Two Pair (Fives and Fours) with a King kicker. Since the board uses 4 cards, the strength is determined by the highest kicker. I lose to any Ace-high or pocket pair (66+), and I chop with any other King-high (as the kicker T doesn't play yet unless the river is lower than a T, but currently the 5th card is the K). I beat all Q-high and lower hands. Leading out (donk betting) is unnecessary as it folds out worse hands (bluffs) that might bet again or allows better hands to call/raise. Checking is standard to induce bluffs or get to a cheap showdown.

The board has double-paired with fives and fours (5c-4h-5d-4s). My Ten-high is currently very weak, losing to any Ace-high or any pocket pair. Pro called my flop bet, which suggests they have some part of the board or a drawing hand. By checking behind on the turn, I avoid being check-raised and can see the river for free, potentially improving to a Ten or Seven or winning if Pro is also holding low cards.

The board is double-paired (5-5-4-4-J), making my hand effectively Five and Fours with a King kicker. I beat all non-Ace high hands (Queen-high and lower) and counterfeited pocket pairs (22, 33). I lose to any Ace-high (kickers play), any pair (66+), or any Jack (which now makes a higher two pair, Jacks and Fives). The opponent checked back the turn, which often indicates marginal showdown value like Ace-high or a small pair, or a give-up. Leading out has no value because worse hands (Q-high) will fold, and better hands (Ax, Jx, pairs) will call. Checking is the optimal line to realize my showdown value against his bluffs/missed hands or to induce a bluff, though calling a bet would be difficult given the likelihood of him holding an Ace.

The board has double-paired with fives and fours (5c-4h-5d-4s), and a Jack appeared on the river. My Ten-high (T-7) is extremely weak at showdown, as any Ace-high or pocket pair (22, 33, 66+) beats me. However, Pro check-called the flop and then checked the turn and river. Since I checked behind on the turn, my range looks capped. A bluff here is risky because Pro's flop check-call often includes Ace-high hands or small pairs that might not fold to a single river bet on such a static board. I will check and take the showdown value, acknowledging that I likely lose to an Ace-high.

In a heads-up match, Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong hand (well within the top 20% of hands). Playing from the Button (Small Blind), I have the positional advantage and should look to raise to take the initiative and build the pot. Limping is too passive and folding is out of the question with this holding. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard, low-risk way to apply pressure and define the pot size, consistent with my previous play in Hand #2.

I am in the Big Blind holding JTo. The Button has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. JTo is well ahead of a standard button opening range and plays well post-flop. I will call to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high on a paired board of 9-6-9 with a flush draw. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them and see how they react to this texture. My hand has some value as two overcards and a backdoor straight/flush possibility, but it is not strong enough to lead out.

I hold Ace-Eight offsuit on a paired board of 9-6-9 with a flush draw. As the pre-flop raiser in position, I have the option to c-bet, but this board texture interacts well with the Big Blind's calling range (trips, pairs, straight draws, flush draws). Ace-high has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot, and betting creates a risk of being check-raised, which would force me to fold a hand that is likely ahead. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets or call a bet on the turn if the card is favorable. I also hold the 8c, giving me backdoor flush potential.

On the turn Queen of Spades, I have picked up an open-ended straight draw (8s and Ks to complete the sequence with 9-T-J-Q). The flop went check-check, suggesting my opponent likely does not have a 9 or a strong pocket pair. The Queen is a good card for me to represent, as I could have hit top pair or be semi-bluffing my draw. I will bet 50% of the pot to try and take the pot down now or build it for when I hit my straight.

I hold Ace-Eight offsuit (Ace-high) on a board of 9c-6c-9h-Qs. After I checked back the flop, my opponent has led out for 200 into a pot of 400 (half-pot) on the turn. In a heads-up match, checking back the flop often induces the opponent to bet the turn with a wide range, including draws (flush or straight draws like 78/JT), small pairs, or pure bluffs, attempting to steal the pot. My Ace-high has significant showdown value against these bluffs. The Queen is an overcard that connects with some of the opponent's range, but folding Ace-high immediately to a standard probe bet makes me too exploitable. The price is good (calling 200 to win 600, requiring ~25% equity), and I have position to re-evaluate on the river. I will call to realize my showdown value.

I missed my open-ended straight draw on the river as the deuce is a complete brick. I bet the turn representing the Queen or a nine, but the opponent's call suggests they have some showdown value, likely a Queen, a six, or a middle pocket pair. Since my Jack-high has no showdown value and a second barrel on the river is unlikely to fold out a hand that called the turn, I will check and concede the pot unless they also have a missed draw.

I hold Ace-Eight offsuit (Ace-high) on a final board of 9c-6c-9h-Qs-2s. The flush draws and straight draws (7-8, J-T) have missed. The opponent bet the turn and checked the river. This line often indicates a missed draw giving up, or a marginal hand like a pair of sixes or a small pocket pair trying to get to showdown cheaply. My Ace-high beats all the missed draws. Betting here would likely only fold out hands I beat and get called by hands that beat me (like a 6 or a Q). Therefore, there is no value in betting. Checking back maximizes my Expected Value (EV) by realizing my showdown equity against their bluffs.
K8o is a strong hand in heads-up play from the button. I'll raise to 300 to exert pressure and build the pot with initiative, which is a standard play for this range of hands.

I hold 94o in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. This is a very weak hand, well below the standard defense range, especially against a larger opening size of 3bb. Being out of position with a disconnected, unsuited hand makes it difficult to realize equity. Although the pot odds are roughly 33%, the reverse implied odds and playability issues make this a clear fold.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Queen-Nine offsuit (Qh 9d). In heads-up play, this is a well-above-average hand (top ~33%). Standard strategy dictates opening a wide range of hands from the button (often 80%+) to steal the blind and play pots in position. Folding is too conservative. While limping is an option, a min-raise to 200 is generally superior as it builds the pot while I have the positional advantage and folds out weak hands from the Big Blind immediately. Given the effective stack depth of roughly 90BB, a min-raise is an efficient size.

I have pocket nines (9c 9s) in the big blind, which is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. The opponent raised to 200 from the small blind/button. I will 3-bet to 600 to build the pot and gain initiative, as my hand is significantly ahead of a standard button opening range.

I am facing a 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind after opening to 200. The pot odds require me to call 400 into a pot of 800 (giving 3:1 or 33% equity needed). While Q9o is a reasonable opening hand heads-up, it performs poorly against a standard 3-betting range, often being dominated by hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, or A9. Although I would have position post-flop, the reverse implied odds and the fact that this is the first 3-bet from the opponent suggest a stronger range. Without a read that the opponent is 3-betting light, folding is the prudent play to preserve the chip lead.

Q9o is a strong hand for heads-up play on the button. I have consistently opened for 300 in previous orbits from this position, and I will continue that sizing to maintain pressure and build the pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability.

I hold 4d 3h in the Big Blind facing a 3bb (300) raise from the Button. This is a very weak off-suit hand (43o) at the bottom of my range. My opponent, Flash, has consistently opened for 3bb rather than a min-raise. To call, I need to contribute 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, requiring about 33% equity realization out of position. 43o struggles to realize equity and is dominated by most of the opponent's opening range. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with Pocket Threes (3c 3d). In Heads-Up play, the Button acts first pre-flop and last post-flop, giving me a positional advantage. Any pair is a decent starter in heads-up, typically ahead of a random range. I have consistently opened to 200 in previous hands (Hand #2, #4, #6). Raising to 200 (min-raise) puts pressure on the Big Blind, prevents them from seeing a free flop, and builds the pot while I have position. Limping is an option but invites aggression from the BB which is hard to defend against with a small pair. Raising allows me to take the initiative.

I am holding QJ offsuit in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up match. Pro opened for 200. To take control of the hand and extract value from weaker hands in Pro's button-opening range, I will 3-bet to 600. This also helps define Pro's hand strength and potentially wins the pot immediately.

I hold a pocket pair (3c 3d) in position. My opponent has 3-bet me to 600. I need to call 400 chips into a pot that will be 1200, giving me 2:1 pot odds (33% equity required). Pocket pairs have reasonably good equity in heads-up play, and with effective stacks of 88bb, I have sufficient implied odds to 'set mine' (attempt to hit three-of-a-kind on the flop). Additionally, since I folded to a similar 3-bet in Hand #6, I need to defend my opening range to prevent my opponent from exploiting me with frequent 3-bets. I will call to utilize my position post-flop and hope to hit a set or a favorable low board.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet to 600, and Pro called. The flop of 4c-6d-8s is fairly dry but can hit some of the medium pocket pairs or connectors in Pro's calling range. However, as the 3-bettor, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet. My QJ-offsuit has two overcards and limited backdoor potential. A 1/3 pot bet (400) is a standard sizing that puts pressure on Pro's missed overcards and small pocket pairs while allowing me to fold without losing too much if I face significant resistance. This also helps define the strength of Pro's hand.

I hold Pocket Threes (3c 3d) on a board of 4c 6d 8s. After calling a pre-flop 3-bet hoping to hit a set ('set mining'), I have missed. The board texture is low but connects with various pairs (88, 66, 44) and the cards are all higher than my pair (underpair). My opponent, who showed strength with the 3-bet, has continued with a c-bet of 400 into a pot of 1600. While the pot odds are attractive (requiring ~20% equity), my hand has poor playability and low showdown value. I am drawing to 2 outs for a set, and practically any turn card is dangerous or strengthens the opponent's range. Continuing here would likely lead to losing more chips on future streets against an aggressive opponent. The disciplined play is to fold after missing the set in a 3-bet pot.

K2s is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, definitely worth an opening raise from the button. I've been raising to 300 consistently in this position, and Pro has folded to this size multiple times already. Maintaining this aggression allows me to pick up the blinds frequently while still having a hand that can flop well.

I am in the Big Blind with QdTh facing a 3BB open raise (300) from the Button. In heads-up play, QTo is a strong hand that falls well within the standard defense range against a Button open. My opponent has been consistently opening to 300, suggesting a standard or slightly aggressive strategy. I need to call 200 into a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity. QTo has significantly more equity than this against a typical wide heads-up opening range. 3-betting is an option, but calling is safer to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid bloating the pot out of position with a medium-strength hand. Therefore, I will call.

I am out of position on a 9-8-6 board with two diamonds. I hold QTo with the Queen of diamonds. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 7), two overcards, and a backdoor flush draw. This board texture hits the Big Blind calling range fairly well, but standard strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is rarely correct here without a specific read or range advantage. I will check and evaluate my opponent's action. If they bet, I have enough equity (gutshot + overcards + BDFD) to consider calling depending on the sizing.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I am continuing with a standard continuation bet. This board (6d-9d-8c) is quite coordinated and likely hits the Big Blind's defending range, but a half-pot bet maintains my aggressive image and forces Pro to have a piece of the board to continue. Given that Pro folded to a flop bet on a similar low, connected board in Hand #8, I want to test if they are willing to defend here.

I am facing a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600) on a wet board of 6d 9d 8c. I hold Qd Th. I have a gutshot straight draw (any 7 makes a straight), two overcards (Queen and Ten), and a backdoor flush draw (Queen of diamonds). The pot odds are very favorable: I need to call 300 to win a total pot of 1500, requiring only 20% equity. Even discounting my overcard outs due to reverse implied odds, the combination of the gutshot and backdoor draws gives me sufficient equity to continue against a wide Button range. Raising is too risky as it isolates me against strong hands, while folding is too tight given the price and my equity. Calling is the optimal play to realize my equity.

I am out of position on the Turn with the board reading 6d 9d 8c Ah. The Ace is a significant card that favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Button) range much more than my calling range. I hold Qd Th, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and a pair of Queens/Tens as outs, though the value of hitting a pair is diminished by the Ace on board. Leading out here would be ill-advised as I lack a range advantage. My goal is to realize my equity (hitting the straight) as cheaply as possible. I will check to the aggressor. If they bet, I will likely have to fold given the strength of the Ace for their range, unless the bet is extremely small.

The turn brings the Ace of Hearts, which is an excellent scare card for me to continue my aggression. As the pre-flop raiser and flop bettor, my range heavily favors having an Ace compared to Pro's defending range. Pro's check on the turn indicates weakness or a marginal hand that was calling the flop (like a mid-pair or a draw). By betting half the pot here, I put significant pressure on those marginal hands and represent a strong Ace, forcing Pro to either have a very strong hand or a high-equity draw to continue.

On the Turn, the board is 6d 9d 8c Ah. I hold Qd Th, which gives me a double gutshot straight draw (outs are four 7s and four Jacks, totaling 8 outs). My opponent bets 600 into a pot of 1200 (half-pot). To call, I need 25% equity. With only one card to come, my 8 outs give me approximately 17.4% equity (8/46). The Ace is a significant scare card that connects very well with the Button's opening and barreling range (Ax hands), reducing the likelihood of a bluff. Additionally, if I hit my straight (especially the 7), the board becomes very coordinated (4-card straight possibilities), which often kills the action and reduces implied odds. Being out of position with insufficient immediate odds and questionable implied odds, folding is the disciplined long-term play.

In a heads-up match, Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o) is a strong starting hand, well above the median. As the Small Blind/Button, I have position post-flop. My standard opening size in previous hands has been a min-raise to 200, which puts pressure on the Big Blind while minimizing risk if faced with a 3-bet (which the opponent has shown a tendency to do). Limping allows the Big Blind to realize equity for free, which is undesirable with high-card hands. Therefore, raising to 200 is the most effective play to define ranges and build the pot with a likely best hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 6c-7h. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 2x (200 chips) from the Small Blind/Button. The pot is currently 300 chips, and it costs me 100 more to call. This gives me 3:1 odds on a call, requiring roughly 25% equity to be profitable. 6-7 offsuit is a standard defending hand against a 2x button open in heads-up play. I will call to see a flop and evaluate, as the price is too good to fold, but the hand is not strong enough to 3-bet for value.

I have flopped middle pair (sevens) on a dry board of Q-4-7. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the most standard play is to check to them. Middle pair is strong enough to call a continuation bet but not strong enough to lead out into the raiser. I'll check and see how Pro reacts.

I hold middle pair (Sevens) with an Ace kicker on a dry, rainbow board (Q-7-4). In position, checking back is a strong option to pot control. It avoids getting check-raised, which would put me in a difficult spot, and induces the opponent to potentially bluff on the turn. The board is not coordinated enough to urgently require protection from draws. This line allows me to comfortably call a turn bet or value bet later if checked to again.

I have middle pair (sevens) with a 6 kicker. The turn 2c is a relatively blank card. On the flop, both players checked, which suggests Pro likely does not have a Queen or is playing a marginal hand cautiously. By checking again on the turn, I am practicing pot control with a medium-strength hand that is likely ahead but vulnerable to being outdrawn by overcards. I also want to induce a bluff or a thin value bet from weaker hands like small pocket pairs or Ace-high. If Pro bets, I plan to call and evaluate the river.

On the turn, I hold middle pair (Sevens) with an Ace kicker. The opponent has checked to me again. While a delayed c-bet could extract value from draws (clubs, straight draws) or worse pairs, checking back is a superior play for pot control. It protects my checking range, avoids facing a check-raise that would force me to fold my equity, and keeps the pot manageable. By checking, I under-represent my hand strength, potentially inducing the opponent to bluff on the river or allowing me to value bet the river if checked to again. The 2c changes little regarding the board texture other than the flush draw, making a check a safe path to showdown.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a board of Q-4-7-2-9. The pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked back on both the flop and the turn, which heavily suggests they do not have a Queen or a strong pocket pair. My hand has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot that hasn't seen any post-flop betting. By checking the river, I am looking to reach a cheap showdown or potentially induce a bluff from missed high cards like AK or AJ. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I don't want to get raised off my marginal holding.

I hold a pair of Sevens (now third pair) with an Ace kicker on a board that has run out with three clubs and an overcard Nine on the river. The opponent has checked to me on all three streets, which signifies a weak range consisting potentially of bottom pairs, missed straight draws, or Ace-highs. However, the arrival of the flush card and the Nine makes value betting very thin; worse hands like 4x or 2x might fold, while better hands like a rivered pair of Nines, a Queen, or a flush will call or check-raise. Checking back guarantees I realize my showdown value against his bluffs and weak pairs without exposing myself to a check-raise or value-owning myself.

I am on the button with Q9o. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play. I have been consistently raising to 3x (300) in previous orbits when I have a playable hand or want to apply pressure. I will continue this strategy to maintain my aggressive image and capitalize on the strength of this hand relative to a random big blind range.

Opponent raises to 3x (300) from the Button/SB. I hold 7h 8s in the Big Blind. The pot odds required to call are 33% (200 to win 600). While 78o has roughly 38-40% raw equity against a wide opening range, realizing that equity out of position against a larger-than-standard raise is difficult. The reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a losing call over the long run against a 3x sizing. I have folded similar weak hands to this specific sizing earlier (Hand 1, 5, 7). Folding is the prudent play here.

I hold Ad 7d on the Button (SB), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play, offering both high card value and flush potential. My opponent, Flash, has been aggressive with 3-bets in previous hands, but A7s is too strong to limp or fold. I will make a standard min-raise to 200 to build the pot in position and take the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. The opponent has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, J4o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given that we are early in the tournament and I have a slight chip lead, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand and risk being put in difficult spots on later streets.

I am on the button with Ace-Four suited, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Historically, I have been aggressive from the button, often opening for 300. Given the strength of my hand and the need to apply pressure to 'Pro', I will raise to my standard sizing of 3x the big blind.

Opponent Flash is opening to 300 (3x) very frequently, suggesting a wide range. I hold J9o in the Big Blind. Against a wide button opening range, J9o has roughly 50-52% equity. I need to call 200 chips to win a total pot of 600, giving me pot odds of 33%. Since my equity is well above the required odds, and I need to defend my blind against frequent aggression to avoid being exploited, calling is the correct play. Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is risky with this specific hand structure out of position.

I hold the 9 of hearts on a monotone board of [3h, 5h, Kh], giving me a middling flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) on this texture would likely only get action from better hands (better flush draws, made flushes, or Top Pair) and fold out the opponent's air, which I want to keep in the pot. I will check and evaluate based on their action, planning to call a standard bet due to my flush draw equity.

I hold Ace-high (As4s) on a monochromatic heart board (3h-5h-Kh). Although I have the Ace of spades, I do not have a heart, making my hand vulnerable to any bet. I am in position and checked to. Taking a free card here is the most prudent move, as it allows me to see the turn without risking more chips on a board that likely favors my opponent's range or puts me in a difficult spot if I'm check-raised. Checking back maintains pot control.

The board now contains four hearts (3h, 5h, Kh, 8h), giving me a 9-high flush. Leading out here is generally poor strategy because it folds out hands I beat (hands without a heart) and builds a pot against hands that crush me (Ah, Qh, Jh, Th). Opponent's flop check-back is ambiguous; he could be trapping with a high heart or have nothing. By checking, I give him a chance to bluff with air or a high card without a heart. I avoid bloating the pot when I am potentially drawing dead to a higher flush, while still retaining showdown value against lower flushes or bluffs.

The board currently shows four hearts, and I do not hold a heart in my hand. My opponent has checked twice, which could indicate they also lack a heart or are potentially trapping with a weak flush. With Ace-high (As4s), I have some showdown value against non-heart hands, but betting into a four-flush board without a heart myself is risky, as I could easily be called by any heart or check-raised. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and maintain pot control.

The river puts 5 hearts on the board [3h, 5h, Kh, 8h, 4h], meaning a flush is possible for anyone, and the board itself is a King-high flush. Holding the 9h, I improve upon the board to a K-9-8-5-4 flush. I beat any opponent holding no hearts (who plays the board) and opponents with lower hearts (7h, 6h, 2h). I lose to opponents holding Ah, Qh, Jh, or Th. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands (board-players fold, low hearts are rare) and isolates me against better flushes. Checking allows me to realize my showdown value against non-heart hands or induce a bluff. Checking is the most prudent play to minimize loss against better flushes while securing the pot against worse holdings.

The board has five hearts, meaning the best 5-card hand is currently a King-high flush. Since I do not hold a heart in my hand, I am playing the board. If my opponent holds any heart higher than the lowest card on the board (which is the 3 of hearts), they will have a superior flush. Pro has checked the river, and after checking the flop and turn, it's likely we are headed for a showdown. Betting here is risky because any heart in the opponent's hand beats me, and they are unlikely to fold a heart or even a split-pot hand for a small bet. Checking back ensures I realize my equity in the split pot or lose the minimum if they have a heart.

I am on the Button (SB) with QJo, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play (top ~17% of hands). My standard strategy is to raise to 2BB (200) to take the initiative and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. My opponent has shown a mix of folding, calling, and 3-betting, but QJo plays well enough post-flop in position to justify an open. Limping is passive and allows the BB to realize equity for free. Therefore, I will min-raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with 6-3 offsuit, an extremely weak starting hand. Pro has opened for 200, which is a standard 2x raise from the button. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, 6-3 offsuit has very poor playability and equity realization, and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position. Conserving my stack for better opportunities is the optimal play here.

I am on the button with 6s 7d. In heads-up play, this is a standard opening hand to maintain pressure and utilize positional advantage. I have been consistently opening for 3x (300) in previous orbits, and I will continue this strategy to remain unpredictable while exploiting the fold equity I have established.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Flash). Flash has been opening this amount frequently (nearly 100% of buttons shown), suggesting a very wide range. Heads-up, K6o has reasonable equity (~52%) against a random hand and performs well enough against a wide opening range to justify a defense, especially getting 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400). While being out of position is a disadvantage, folding a King-high hand to a standard open in heads-up play is too exploitable. I will call and evaluate the flop.
